Valory cut deployment time of their payments and billing infrastructure for the Olas AI agent marketplace from 6 weeks to 6 hours using Nevermined, clawing back $1000s in engineering costs

### Case Study: Valory and the Olas AI Agent Marketplace

Valory cut deployment time of their payments and billing infrastructure for the Olas AI agent marketplace from 6 weeks to 6 hours using Nevermined, clawing back $1000s in engineering costs. CEO David Minarsch stated: "We knew AI agents need to be able to transact, so over a year ago we tapped into Nevermined. Nevermined was, and continues to be, the best solution for AI payments."

## Comprehensive Monitoring and Analytics for AI Agent Performance

Monetizing AI agents requires visibility into both technical performance and business metrics. Nevermined's observability dashboard provides both in a single view. [...] Unified x402 payment handshake
 Usage-driven programmable settlement
 Smart account session key support
 Escrow with conditional release
 Revenue splits across multiple parties

## Rapid Deployment and Integration: Accelerating AI Agent Monetization

Building custom billing infrastructure for AI agents typically requires weeks of development time and ongoing maintenance. Nevermined compresses this to minutes.

### Minutes to Monetization: Nevermined's SDK Advantage

The low-code SDK enables rapid deployment through a three-step process:

1. Install the SDK via npm (TypeScript) or pip (Python)
2. Register payment plans with pricing rules and access controls
3. Validate API requests while tracking costs through the observability layer

## Step 1: Add the MCP server to your gateway

Zuplo has native MCP server support built into the gateway. Adding one takes about 30 seconds.

Go to your project’s route list and click the Add tab. You’ll see an MCP Server option. Select it, and configure the basics:

 Name: `ChangeLoggle MCP Server`
 Version: `0.0.1`
 Description: `Create, update, and discover team changelog info.`

That creates the MCP server endpoint at `/mcp` on your gateway.

#### MCP Servers with Zuplo [...] Monetizing an MCP server means letting paid subscribers connect AI assistants to your API while blocking free users. This is Part 3 of the “Building a Monetized API” series. In Part 1, we set up the API gateway with authentication, consumer isolation, and rate limiting. In Part 2, we added meters, plans, Stripe integration, and a self-serve developer portal. Now we’ll monetize the MCP server on top of the same API with a Custom Code Inbound policy that calls `MonetizationInboundPolicy.getSubscriptionData()` from `@zuplo/runtime` and returns a 403 when the `mcp_server` entitlement is missing.

It's a complete Node.js project. It even [...] you can build MCP servers using Node.js, Python, or Java. You can host your server for life without being billed, regardless of your user count. Your project launches as a SaaS product, and anyone can subscribe to it. You can design your own packages, premium or free, as I showed in that video. When you make a sale, you get an 85% commission, and they take 15%. If it doesn't sell, they don't charge you anything. Yet hosting remains free. It's a very convenient system. In that video, I had to brainstorm the idea, a GitHub issue tracker, and build it manually. But developers are often better at building than ideating. ## 1. Monthly/Annual Subscription

 Each action consumes a different number of credits depending on complexity [...] Press enter or click to view image in full size

Controls who can access your server:

```
import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";import { verifyUser } from "./authentication.js";const server = new McpServer({ name: "YourMCPServer", version: "1.0.0", middleware: async (context, next) => { const authHeader = context.request.headers.get('Authorization'); if (!authHeader) return new Response('Access denied', { status: 401 }); const [, apiKey] = authHeader.split(' '); const user = await verifyUser(apiKey); if (!user) return new Response('Invalid API key', { status: 401 }); context.state.user = user; return next(); }});
```

## 2. Payment Integration

Connect your system to Stripe, LemonSqueezy, or similar to: [...] ```
server.tool("generateImage", { prompt: z.string(), style: z.enum(["realistic", "anime", "painting"]).optional() }, async ({ prompt, style = "realistic" }, context) => { const user = context.state.user; const quality = user.plan === "premium" ? "high" : "standard"; await logUsage(user.id, "generateImage"); if (user.plan === "credits") {ty await deductCredits(user.id, 1); } const imageUrl = await generateWithReplicate(prompt, style, quality); return { content: [{ type: "image", imageUrl, alt: `Image: ${prompt}` }] }; });
```

## User Flow

1. Ana signs up and selects a monthly plan
2. She receives her API key and custom config
3. She writes: “Generate an anime-style image of a space cat”
4. Claude connects to the MCP, the image is generated and shown in the conversation

results.title: Monetizing MCP (Model Context Protocol) Servers with ...
results.content:
## How Moesif Helps Monetize MCP Server Usage

In addition to a sound pricing strategy, you also need visibility, attribution, and control to turn MCP traffic into revenue. Moesif provides the infrastructure to meter, monitor, and monetize MCP server usage with minimal code changes. Let’s break down how Moesif supports each part of the monetization workflow.

### Real-Time Observability for MCP Traffic

Moesif captures each JSON-RPC request hitting your MCP server, including method name, parameters, response status, and latency. This is critical in MCP environments, where AI apps can initiate rapid, multi-step invocations that behave more like background systems than human-facing APIs.

The platform supports several integration methods. You can use server-side SDKs: [...] ## Identifying and Measuring Billable Usage in MCP

Before you can monetize an MCP server, you need to decide what exactly you’re charging for. This means identifying the unit of context or interaction that reflects the cost incurred or value delivered. Because MCP servers are not traditional REST APIs, but rather execution surfaces for agents, they require more deliberate thinking about what to meter.

### Method Calls as the Base Unit

The simplest approach is to charge per JSON-RPC method invocation. Each request maps to a specific tool or function your MCP server exposes. Billing per method is easy to track, especially when agents trigger methods programmatically. It provides a good starting point for services with uniform operational costs. [...] Python (like `moesifasgi` for ASGI-based frameworks like FastAPI)
 Node.js (Express, Koa)
 Java

Alternatively, if your MCP server is fronted by an API gateway like WSO2, Kong, or AWS API Gateway, Moesif’s plugins let you instrument traffic at the edge. These integrations work with asynchronous patterns, including Server-Sent Events (SSE) and Streamable HTTP.

### Billing Meters and Usage Attribution
